question: What are the top 5 ways a determined user could bypass your tool? How would you detect those bypass attempts?
title: Bypass Vectors and Detection Resilience
bullets: Enumerate realistic bypass techniques across different layers (client, network, API, endpoint)
Explain how each bypass attempt is detected, including telemetry and signals
Quantify detection reliability and known blind spots
Reference threat modeling practices and documented assumptions
suggested_inputs: Threat model documentation with in-scope and out-of-scope attacks
Detection rules or correlation logic for bypass attempts
Logs or alerts showing bypass detection scenarios
Hardening guidelines for preventing or limiting bypass techniques

good.text: Top bypass methods include: direct API calls via curl or scripts, use of unmanaged devices, disabling browser extensions or agents, routing traffic outside enforced proxies, and offline data exfiltration. We detect these via anomaly detection, missing telemetry signals, device posture checks, and proxy evasion alerts. These are documented in our threat model and deployment guide.

Users can bypass controls by uninstalling the agent or using alternative access paths like personal devices. We detect this with ~80–90% reliability using heartbeat monitoring, device compliance checks, and traffic correlation. However, sophisticated adversaries using encrypted tunnels or air-gapped methods may evade detection.

We assume bypass attempts will occur and design layered detection: endpoint signals (agent status), network anomalies (unexpected traffic patterns), and identity-based inconsistencies. Each bypass vector is mapped to a detection mechanism with defined coverage and limitations.

Our threat model explicitly lists bypass scenarios such as proxy evasion, agent tampering, and alternate API usage. For each, we define detection strategies and clearly state which scenarios are out of scope without additional controls.

partial.text: Users might bypass controls by using different devices or tools, and we try to monitor such behavior where possible.
We have monitoring systems in place to detect unusual activity, including potential bypass attempts.
We consider bypass scenarios in our design, but details depend on customer configuration and deployment.
We can detect most bypass attempts, though exact coverage and reliability are not formally measured.

bad.text: Our tool cannot be bypassed under any circumstances.
We have never seen any bypass attempts in our system.
Users typically don’t try to bypass controls, so this is not a concern.
We are not aware of specific ways users could bypass our tool.

follow_ups: If a user disables or uninstalls your agent or extension, how quickly do you detect the loss of coverage and what alert is generated?
Can users operate in private or stealth modes that bypass your monitoring? How do you detect such behavior?
Show your formal threat model and identify which bypass techniques are in-scope versus out-of-scope.
Demonstrate a real scenario where a bypass attempt was detected and how your system responded.
